source_attribution: Distilled from 100+ seller posts, not customer-specific PII

entries.scenario: Customer says item arrived broken, no photo attached, under $50.
entries.decision: Auto-refund without requesting photo. Cost of requesting evidence ($1.50 in agent time + 8% churn) exceeds fraud risk on items under $50.

entries.scenario: Customer claims SNAD (significantly not as described) on apparel.
entries.decision: Refund without requiring return for items under $35. Return shipping + restocking labor > refund value for soft goods.

entries.scenario: Customer says they received the wrong item.
entries.decision: Ship correct item immediately. Do NOT ask them to return the wrong one first — it doubles the time-to-resolution and amplifies the original mistake.

entries.scenario: Repeated complaint about the same order (3rd+ contact).
entries.decision: Escalate to senior, regardless of amount. Repeat contact is the strongest churn signal in e-commerce CS data.

entries.scenario: Account has 5+ damage claims in 60 days on different items.
entries.decision: Flag for fraud review before any refund. Repeat-claim pattern is the #1 indicator of return abuse.

entries.scenario: Tracking shows delivered but customer says not received.
entries.decision: Refund or replace on first claim if order < $100. Past $100, ask customer to file with carrier first and check with neighbors / building manager. Most 'not received' is misdelivery within 50m.

entries.scenario: Customer is a Prime member with high order history (>$5k LTV).
entries.decision: Approve any reasonable refund up to $200 without question, no return. Cost of losing a Prime customer is 18 months of equivalent margin.

entries.scenario: Customer asks for refund after 30 days but within 45.
entries.decision: Honor it once per customer per year. The marginal goodwill from saying yes outweighs the 5% additional refund cost; saying no on a borderline case is the most common driver of negative reviews.

entries.scenario: Subscribe-and-Save customer cancels after first auto-delivery, claims didn't realize.
entries.decision: Full refund + cancel + brief explanation. Do NOT defend the policy. Each defensive reply tanks the seller rating; the refund is cheaper than two 1-star reviews.

entries.scenario: Refund issued — customer asks when they'll see it.
entries.decision: Always cite '1-3 business days for credit cards, 5-7 for ACH/bank transfer'. Vague timelines = repeat tickets. Specificity is the cheapest customer service.

entries.scenario: Buyer opens 'item not as described' case on a used item.
entries.decision: Offer partial refund first (typically 20-40% of item value); accept full return as fallback. Used-item full-return shipping often exceeds resale value.

entries.scenario: Buyer received item damaged with no insurance on shipment.
entries.decision: Seller eats the cost. Buyer-side experience matters; recover via shipper claim on your end. Never tell the buyer 'you didn't pick insurance'.

entries.scenario: Buyer claims missing parts; auction was sold as 'parts only'.
entries.decision: Re-read listing carefully. If listing explicitly says 'no warranty, parts only', politely point to that line and decline. Otherwise refund. Listing accuracy = your defense.

entries.scenario: Buyer opens a case before messaging.
entries.decision: Reply within 4 hours even if you can't resolve yet. Response time matters more than first-message resolution; eBay's defect rate weights response speed heavily.

entries.scenario: Buyer returns a different item than what was sold.
entries.decision: Document with photos before refunding. Open a counter-claim with eBay (Seller Protection). Do not refund preemptively; the platform will side with whoever has photo evidence.

entries.scenario: International buyer claims package never arrived.
entries.decision: Without tracking ID with delivery confirmation, refund. With tracking confirming delivery, ask buyer to check with local postal service first; provide claim form for their carrier.

entries.scenario: Repeat buyer asks for discount on damage instead of refund.
entries.decision: Honor it — gives you discretion on amount. 10-20% partial refund saves you the return and binds the buyer to future purchases.

entries.scenario: Buyer wants to return for reason 'changed mind' on item marked 'No Returns'.
entries.decision: You can refuse, but offering anyway (20% partial, no return) often prevents a SNAD case. SNAD bypasses your No-Returns policy entirely.

entries.scenario: Customer wants refund as eBay gift card / store credit.
entries.decision: Most customers accept gift card when it's offered with a small bonus ($5-10 on top). Cheaper than cash refund + preserves margin.

entries.scenario: Customer claims item arrived but is 'defective in some way' without specifics.
entries.decision: Always ask for one clarifying question + photo before deciding. Vague claims have 40% higher fraud rate; ask for evidence respectfully.

entries.scenario: Customer requests refund after using 'final sale' item.
entries.decision: Honor it for first-time customers; politely decline with empathy for repeat. Final-sale is enforceable, but the lifetime value math favors flexibility on first encounter.

entries.scenario: Refunding multi-item order — only one item damaged.
entries.decision: Refund the damaged line item + a prorated share of shipping. Do not refund whole order shipping unless customer is returning everything.

entries.scenario: Customer is angry — first message contains profanity.
entries.decision: Mirror their concern, not their language. Open with 'You're absolutely right to be frustrated' or equivalent acknowledgment. De-escalation cuts time-to-resolution by 60%.

entries.scenario: Pre-order item delayed beyond promised date.
entries.decision: Offer cancellation + 10% credit if they keep waiting. Don't make them ask; proactively message at the delay boundary.

entries.scenario: Customer in EU wants return shipping prepaid for change-of-mind return.
entries.decision: EU law (Distance Selling Directive) requires it for 14-day returns. Not optional.

entries.scenario: Customer's order was lost in transit, they want immediate replacement + tracking.
entries.decision: Ship the replacement first, file the carrier claim in parallel. Customer-waiting > carrier-reimbursement timeline.

entries.scenario: Chargeback filed by customer who also has open refund request with us.
entries.decision: Always respond to chargeback with full evidence regardless; provide tracking + signed delivery + email trail. Fighting chargebacks recovers ~30% on average.

entries.scenario: Customer requests refund to a different card than they purchased with.
entries.decision: Decline; refund must go to original payment method (PCI requirement). Offer store credit as alternative.

entries.scenario: Customer wants refund on personalized / custom product.
entries.decision: Manufacturing defect → full refund. 'Changed mind' on customized → decline politely, offer store credit goodwill (small).

entries.scenario: Long email thread, customer keeps adding new complaints.
entries.decision: Address each numbered item in your reply explicitly. Skipping any one issue triggers a 'you didn't read my message' escalation.

entries.scenario: First-time customer's first order has a problem.
entries.decision: Over-resolve. The cost of a $25 over-refund is much less than the LTV of a repeat customer who tells their friends.

entries.scenario: Customer asks for refund 90 days after delivery.
entries.decision: Policy says 30 days; honor anyway as one-time goodwill for first request, note the account. Defending policy on borderline cases generates the most negative reviews.

entries.scenario: Customer writes a long emotional message about how the product was for their late parent.
entries.decision: Acknowledge the emotional context BEFORE the resolution. 'I'm so sorry — I can hear how much this meant. Here's what I'm doing...' Empathy buys patience for the actual fix.

entries.scenario: Customer demands refund + keeps the item AND threatens chargeback.
entries.decision: Give in if amount is under $50; the chargeback fee + dispute time is $25-40 plus 30 days of work. Document the threat for fraud database.

entries.scenario: Multiple customers report same product arriving damaged this month.
entries.decision: Pause shipment, audit packaging at the warehouse, update packaging spec. Reactive refunds compound; root-cause once.

entries.scenario: Customer wants 'price adjustment' because item went on sale after purchase.
entries.decision: Honor within 14 days as store credit (not cash). Builds trust, encourages return purchase. Beyond 14 days, decline politely.

entries.scenario: Customer keeps asking 'when will it ship?' for a backorder.
entries.decision: Send a single proactive update with exact ship date even if it's 2 weeks out. Silence creates anxiety; transparency reduces inquiries by 70%.

entries.scenario: Calculating whether to ask for return.
entries.decision: If (refund value < return shipping × 2 + 10 min of warehouse handling), do NOT ask for return. Process the refund and move on.

entries.scenario: Choosing between full refund and replacement.
entries.decision: Replacement preserves margin (only cost = COGS + shipping) vs refund (loses full revenue). Default to replacement for fragile/seasonal/perishable; default to refund for customer-pick errors.

entries.scenario: Setting an auto-approve threshold for damage claims.
entries.decision: Median small DTC brand: $35-50 auto-approve. Above that, require photo. Above $200, require human review. Sweet spot trades off fraud loss vs CS labor cost.

entries.scenario: Customer in escalated state wants compensation beyond standard refund.
entries.decision: Small store credit ($5-15) added to refund saves the relationship. Larger goodwill (>$25) only with manager approval — sets precedent customer expects repeated.

entries.scenario: Item is on a 'final sale' page but customer says photos showed wrong color.
entries.decision: Honor SNAD claim; final-sale doesn't override marketing accuracy. Refund/return as if not final sale; flag listing for review.

entries.scenario: Customer wants to skip a Subscribe-and-Save shipment that already shipped.
entries.decision: Refund the box once it arrives; tell them clearly they can keep or donate the item. Telling them to refuse delivery creates UPS handling cost.

entries.scenario: Holiday gift purchased in November, recipient wants to return in February.
entries.decision: Honor it within 'extended holiday window' (typically Nov-Jan purchases get until Jan 31). Communicate the window proactively in shipping emails.

entries.scenario: Customer's English isn't strong; sentences are short and unclear.
entries.decision: Reply in clear short sentences with one question at a time. Don't dump a list of options. Translation tools often miss conditionals.

entries.scenario: Same delivery address has had 12 'item not received' claims across 8 accounts in 6 months.
entries.decision: Block address. Carrier-level fraud ring. Notify carrier investigations team.

entries.scenario: Customer claims auction item was 'much smaller than expected'.
entries.decision: Check your listing — did you include dimensions and a sense-of-scale photo (with a coin, hand, or ruler)? If not, refund; update listing template.

entries.scenario: Bidder won an auction but later regrets it.
entries.decision: Mutually cancel transaction (eBay calls it 'cancel order' — buyer initiated). Don't fight; bad-feedback risk > the 12% relisting cost.

entries.scenario: Customer wants exchange for different size of clothing item.
entries.decision: Process as exchange (no refund cycle). Ship the new size immediately, send return label for the original. Faster + cheaper than refund + new order.

entries.scenario: Customer reports product mismatch after months of using item.
entries.decision: Investigate; if listing was inaccurate, partial credit (10-30%) makes sense. Full refund after months of use is rare unless there's a defect.

entries.scenario: Customer writes a 5-star review but also requests a refund for one minor issue.
entries.decision: Honor the refund quickly; thank them explicitly for the review. They're an advocate — invest in keeping them.

entries.scenario: New customer, expensive first order, immediate refund request claiming defect.
entries.decision: Process if photo is convincing, but flag the account in fraud system. Pattern: 30%+ of 'big first order + immediate defect' is return abuse.

entries.scenario: Customer asks: 'are you a bot?'
entries.decision: Answer truthfully and pivot to the resolution. 'You're talking to an AI assistant, and I can resolve refunds up to $50 instantly. For anything bigger, my colleague takes over.' Transparency builds trust; deception erodes it.

entries.scenario: Should we tell the customer we're an AI?
entries.decision: Yes, if asked. Don't volunteer it unprompted (creates 'is this even worth my time' bias). Be ready to escalate transparently.

entries.scenario: Setting tone for refund decline.
entries.decision: Never start with 'unfortunately'. Lead with what you CAN do: 'Here's what I can do — I can offer a 20% store credit, or arrange a return for full refund.' Frame the option, not the limitation.

entries.scenario: Customer in same Prime household made the order; primary account holder asks for refund.
entries.decision: Process for the primary holder — they own the payment method. Privacy law doesn't allow you to refund a third party without explicit consent.

entries.scenario: Customer message contains an attached photo of the damage AND a separate angry text.
entries.decision: Lead the reply with what you see in the photo, not the text. 'I can see the crack along the rim...' Acknowledges they did their part (sent evidence) and shows you actually looked.

entries.scenario: Customer claims they were charged twice.
entries.decision: Always check first (90% are pending auths that drop in 3-5 days). If real double charge, refund immediately + apologize; the trust damage is severe.

entries.scenario: Box arrived crushed, customer doesn't know if contents are damaged yet.
entries.decision: Tell them to open carefully and inspect; if any damage to contents, you'll refund/replace immediately, no questions. Sets expectations + accelerates trust.

entries.scenario: Item shows 'delivered' but customer in apartment complex says they didn't get it.
entries.decision: Most likely lobby / wrong unit. Ask them to check with neighbors and concierge before you ship a replacement. If first response, replace anyway under $50.

entries.scenario: Customer wants to extend return window because they were traveling.
entries.decision: One-time extension of up to 30 days, no questions. Note account; pattern of repeated 'I was traveling' is a signal.

entries.scenario: Buyer leaves negative feedback before contacting you.
entries.decision: Respond on the feedback publicly with professional fix offer. Then DM the resolution. Both audiences (this buyer + future buyers) are watching.

entries.scenario: Customer wants partial refund for slightly delayed delivery (1-2 days).
entries.decision: Decline politely unless they paid for express. 'Delivery estimates are estimates' is enforceable; constant partials for minor delays sets bad precedent.

entries.scenario: Customer says other brand handled similar issue better.
entries.decision: Don't defend; ask what specifically would feel right to them. Often they want acknowledgment + a small extra (free shipping next time, $5 credit). Cheaper than the comparison.

entries.scenario: Choosing between issuing a refund vs giving extra store credit.
entries.decision: Store credit costs you ~60% of refund value (only paid when redeemed; some unredeemed). Refund costs you 100%. Offer store credit + small bonus as preferred default.

entries.scenario: Cross-border order, customer wants return shipping reimbursed.
entries.decision: Calculate vs item value. If return shipping > 30% of item, just refund without return. Cross-border returns also have customs hassle for receiver.

entries.scenario: Customer claims item was empty box — but tracking shows correct weight.
entries.decision: Politely ask for a photo of the box exterior + interior. Suggest they may have received the wrong box; offer to investigate with carrier. Don't accuse; let evidence steer.

entries.scenario: Customer message is in a language you don't speak.
entries.decision: Use translation + reply in their language. Mark the reply 'auto-translated; let me know if anything's unclear'. Better than English-only.

entries.scenario: Customer leaves passing comment about a future product line they wish you'd make.
entries.decision: Acknowledge it, thank them, route to product team. Costs nothing, customer feels heard, occasionally produces real product insight.

entries.scenario: Customer wants refund without returning, but item is fully functional.
entries.decision: Decline refund; offer return for refund. Functional items that customer keeps + refund creates double-spend risk (sells the item, profits).

entries.scenario: Setting refund window for digital products.
entries.decision: 7-day refund window on digital — long enough for honest evaluation, short enough to discourage rip-and-return abuse.

entries.scenario: Customer requests partial refund as alternative to full return.
entries.decision: Calculate: 30% of item value typically covers customer dissatisfaction + saves return logistics + lets you keep ~70% revenue. Sweet spot for most categories.

entries.scenario: Whether to apologize when fault isn't yours (carrier damage).
entries.decision: Yes — own the customer's experience, not the cause. 'I'm sorry the package arrived this way' (not 'I'm sorry our partner damaged it'). Internal blame helps no one.

entries.scenario: When to invest in proactive communication.
entries.decision: Anytime you discover an issue before customer does (delay, stock out, recall): message them with action plan. Costs $0; reduces inbound tickets by ~40%.

entries.scenario: Setting price for 'goodwill gesture' after a service failure.
entries.decision: Industry rule of thumb: 1-2× the inconvenience. Late by a day = next order free shipping. Wrong item = original cost + a $10 credit. Larger gestures encourage repeated complaints.

entries.scenario: Frequency to review and update CS policies.
entries.decision: Quarterly. CS data accumulates patterns (new product issues, fraud trends, seasonal effects). Stale policies cost more than the meeting time.

entries.scenario: Customer writes 'I want my money back' with no other detail.
entries.decision: Reply with: '(1) order number, (2) which item, (3) what's wrong, (4) photo if applicable'. Numbered list = highest response rate. Don't refund blind.

entries.scenario: Damaged item is a perishable about to spoil.
entries.decision: Refund immediately, no return. Spoilage during dispute resolution is wasted time + product. Treat perishables on the half-life of the product.

entries.scenario: Buyer paid via Managed Payments, refund pulls from your balance.
entries.decision: Know your balance. If insufficient, eBay will charge your bank — but with delay. Maintain 7-10 days of typical refund volume as buffer.

entries.scenario: Buyer files a return then doesn't ship the item back.
entries.decision: Wait the eBay-mandated window, then automatically close the case in your favor when system allows. eBay treats it as resolved.

entries.scenario: Customer says shipping label was generated weeks ago but tracking never updated.
entries.decision: Carrier likely lost the item before scanning. Refund or reship immediately; don't make customer wait for carrier investigation.

entries.scenario: Customer is happy with the resolution but adds 'next time can you ship in better packaging?'
entries.decision: Thank them, mark the suggestion in your packaging review queue. Direct customer feedback is the highest-quality product input you'll get.

entries.scenario: Customer asks for refund AND a discount on their next purchase.
entries.decision: Pick one or the other based on context. Both = bad precedent. If they were genuinely wronged, refund. If they were inconvenienced, small future discount.

entries.scenario: Customer's complaint includes a question about an unrelated thing (e.g., size chart).
entries.decision: Answer both in your reply. Don't tell them to 'open a separate ticket'. Each ticket is a touch point; combining them is faster + more humane.

entries.scenario: Setting the threshold above which a human must review.
entries.decision: Variable by margin profile. High-margin (>50%) brands can auto-approve to $200. Low-margin grocery / commodity needs to drop to $30. Set by cost of error tolerance.

entries.scenario: How to phrase a decline when policy says no.
entries.decision: 1) Acknowledge what they want. 2) Explain ONE concrete reason (not policy quote). 3) Offer the next-best thing you CAN do. 4) Invite them to escalate if dissatisfied. Never just refuse with citations.

entries.scenario: Customer mentions they're a small business owner buying for resale.
entries.decision: Offer to introduce them to your B2B / wholesale program if you have one. Higher-volume + lower-touch customer.

entries.scenario: Buyer says they didn't read the listing carefully and bought wrong item.
entries.decision: Honor return as buyer-remorse; charge return shipping unless your policy says free returns. Empathy without footing the bill for buyer error.

entries.scenario: Item arrived in different condition than the customer expected (color shade).
entries.decision: Send a single high-quality color photo. Often resolves the issue. If not, offer return for full refund (color SNAD is hard to dispute).

entries.scenario: Frequent return-and-refund pattern from same customer (5+ in 90 days).
entries.decision: Quietly add to 'limited return privileges' list. Don't announce it; communicate selectively on future returns ('unfortunately this one requires return shipping at customer expense').

entries.scenario: Resolving an ambiguous claim (photo unclear, customer description vague).
entries.decision: Default to customer-favorable resolution for items under $50; ask 1 specific question for items $50-200; escalate to human above $200. Friction proportional to fraud risk.

entries.scenario: Customer asks for compensation in addition to refund — for 'wasted time'.
entries.decision: Decline cash compensation for time. Offer small store credit ($5-10) for goodwill. Cash for time sets a dangerous precedent and is unbounded.

entries.scenario: Customer's claim doesn't match any specific policy.
entries.decision: Apply the 'fair coffee shop' test — what would a small business owner with the customer in front of them do? Usually that's the answer.
